3.2 Vasomotor responses 3.2.1 Resistance vessels Acetylcholine, adenosine and nitroglycerin all caused coronary resistance vessel vasodilatation and an increase in coronary artery blood flow ( P 0.002; Fig. 2 ) with adenosine causing the largest increase ( P <0.004 vs. other agents). There were no differences in the resistance vessel responses between the three remodeling groups.
View larger version (30K): [in this window] [in a new window] [Download PowerPoint slide] Fig. 2 Baseline (white bars) and peak (black bars) coronary blood flow (percentage change, gray bars) to intracoronary acetylcholine, adenosine and nitroglycerin. ANOVA for comparison of percentage increase in blood flow for the four drug boluses. Paired t -test for comparison of baseline and peak blood flow for each drug bolus.
3.2.2 Conduit vessels All vessel segments vasodilated to adenosine and nitroglycerin, and vasoconstricted to 10 –4 M acetylcholine ( P <0.05 for all; Fig. 3 ). The vasomotor response to acetylcholine 10 –6 M varied according to the category of vascular remodeling ( P <0.001; Fig. 3 ). Non-remodeled segments had a greater change in vessel cross-sectional area with 10 –6 M acetylcholine (4.9±0.8%), compared to positively and negatively remodeled segments (0.6±1.8 and –4.9±1.8%, respectively, P <0.05). A significant degree of preservation of vasodilatation to 10 –6 M acetylcholine was evident in positively remodeled compared with negatively remodeled segments ( P <0.05). There was a trend towards greater adenosine-induced vasodilatation in non-remodeled segments (increase in vessel cross-sectional area of 6.2±1.0%) compared with positively (3.7±0.8%) and negatively (3.9±0.9%) remodeled segments ( P = 0.14, Fig. 3 ). Similarly, nitroglycerin caused greater vasodilatation in non-remodeled segments (increase in vessel cross-sectional area of 7.2±3.8%) than either positively (4.7±0.9%) or negatively (3.7±0.6%) remodeled segments ( P <0.01, Fig. 3 ).

ANOVA for comparison of remodeled segment types. * P <0.05 ( t -test; negatively remodeled segments vs. positively or non-remodeled segments).
4. Discussion
Top Abstract 1. Introduction 2. Methods 3. Results 4. Discussion 5. Conclusions References We have demonstrated that, in keeping with a more fibrotic and vasospastic response to atheroma deposition, negatively remodeled coronary arterial segments are stiffer and less distensible. Moreover, we have shown that the type of vascular remodeling determines the local epicardial vasomotor response to both endothelium-dependent and -independent vasodilators. In particular, negatively remodeled segments have an exaggerated vasoconstrictor response to acetylcholine and impaired endothelium-independent vasodilatation. We conclude that negative remodeling is associated with more pronounced local vascular and endothelial dysfunction.
4.1 Endothelial function At the lowest concentration of acetycholine, there was preservation of endothelium-dependent vasodilatation in non-remodeled segments and, to a lesser extent, positively remodeled segments. However, in negatively remodeled segments, low dose acetylcholine caused vasoconstriction that was equivalent to that observed with high dose acetylcholine in all segments. The phenomenon of paradoxical epicardial vasoconstriction during acetylcholine administration in atherosclerotic coronary arteries was first described 15 years ago [20] . It has been attributed to the direct muscarinic vasoconstrictor action of acetylcholine on the vascular smooth muscle cells in the absence of functional endothelium-dependent vasodilatation. Consistent with previous work [21] , our observation of low dose acetylcholine-induced vasoconstriction cannot be directly attributed to the atherosclerotic plaque burden since both positively and negatively remodeled segments had a similar plaque load but distinct vasomotor responses. It may, in part, be explained by the reduced vasodilatation response to the endothelium-independent vasodilator, nitroglycerin, suggesting impaired vascular smooth muscle sensitivity to nitric oxide in remodeled segments. However, this cannot be the only explanation since the reduced response to nitroglycerin was observed in both positively and negatively remodeled segments but only negatively remodeled segments demonstrated vasoconstriction to low dose acetylcholine. This suggests that negative remodeling is associated with endothelial dysfunction or alterations in muscarinic receptor responsiveness.
4.2 Vascular remodeling The current finding of an association between negative remodeling and vascular dysfunction does not address the question of cause and effect. It is tempting to speculate that localized vascular and endothelial dysfunction may lead to a more vasospastic artery that would cause increased vessel stiffness and negative remodeling. Alternatively, these features may have a common etiology with the potentially more fibrotic and inflammatory reaction to atheroma formation not only leading to negative remodeling but also vascular and endothelial dysfunction. In this regard, longitudinal studies would be of value to monitor the changes in vascular function and vessel remodeling over time in order to determine which is the dominant factor in determining these vascular effects. This would also determine if vessels negatively remodel from the beginning or go through an initial phase of positive remodeling which becomes exhausted through progressive endothelial dysfunction and fibrosis [22] . In a hypercholesterolemic rabbit model, endothelial dysfunction was associated with negative remodeling and restenosis after balloon injury implicating endothelial dysfunction in the causation of negative remodeling [23] .
Irrespective of the causative relationship, the present study suggests that there are functional abnormalities associated with coronary artery remodeling in addition to anatomical and structural changes. There has been only one previous study to address this issue. Lerman and colleagues used quantitative coronary angiography to determine epicardial responses to 10 –4 M acetylcholine and nitroglycerin in the left anterior descending coronary artery [9] . This study used a single dose of acetylcholine and employed a different classification of vascular remodeling. However, consistent with our current findings, Lerman and co-workers suggested that positive remodeling is associated with endothelial dysfunction. We have now extended these observations by providing more detailed assessments of remodeled segments, and demonstrating that negative remodeling is associated with more pronounced endothelium-dependent and -independent vasomotor abnormalities.
4.2.1 Study limitations This study was conducted in the necessary clinical setting of patients with a combination of risk factors and concomitant therapies undergoing diagnostic coronary angiography. The modest sample size means that this study lacks sufficient power to address the influence of all the individual variables associated with coronary artery disease. The study was also performed at a single time point and temporal changes in the remodeling process are unknown.
We defined the remodeling segment with reference to proximal and distal segments. Because of the clinical setting of this study, some of these segments will have incorporated some mild degree of atherosclerotic plaque: the so-called ‘near-normal’ segments. Because this could theoretically affect the classification of the remodeling segments, we avoided arterial segments where the remodeling classification was unclear. It should also be recognised that our findings may not be representative of all coronary artery atherosclerotic plaques. Heavily calcified lesions were excluded because the resultant acoustic shadow makes it very difficult to measure vessel areas. In addition, whilst we excluded segments with rotational, angular or image artifacts, we cannot completely eliminate these effects and recognise this as a potential limitation.
5. Conclusions
Top Abstract 1. Introduction 2. Methods 3. Results 4. Discussion 5. Conclusions References Vascular remodeling is an important and major determinant of epicardial vasomotor responses. Both structural and functional abnormalities are associated with negative remodeling that may contribute to the adverse effects of such lesions.
Time for primary review 32 days.

Several things should be explained. There is a blue coral in the local waters, but it could hardly have been made into beads, nor are any such known. "Coral" is the word for bead in several languages, including Dutch, from whom many of these writers got their information. These travelers were no better at distinguishing glass from stones as many people are today.
The name of the bead changed over time. In the 16 th century it was cori , becoming accori in the 17 th (the Fante, who live near El Mina, tend to put a vowel at the beginning of a word starting with a consonant). In the early 18 th century, both Barbot and Bosman say that the natives called the bead accori , but that the Europeans called it aggrey , which became the standard name.
As long as the beads were being imported, the source was always to the east of Ghana. Importing had stopped by the early 19 th century and the sources were then in Ghana. The Phoenician origin was based on the mistaken impression that chevrons , thought then to be Phoenician, were Aggrey beads.
The reprehensible Bowdich threw the most mud into people's eyes. His account was widely read and his description of "aggrey" beads was simply a list of all the important beads the Asante (Ashanti) owned. After him, chevrons and all sorts of other beads were mistakenly called "aggrey."
Up until Bowdich, nearly everyone agreed that the bead was blue. Brun took a closer look and saw it was dichroic: blue by reflected light and green by transmitted light. This was echoed by the Portuguese Emmanuel Ximenes, who asked the great glassmaker Antonio Neri in the early 16 th century how to make dichroic blue/green glass to imitate a "stone" bead popular on the Gold Coast (Zecchin 1964:24)
What does archaeology tell us? Davison et al. (1971) analyzed drawn dichroic blue/green beads found in old trans-Saharan trading cities (Gao, Kubi Salah, Tegdaoust, etc.) and at Ife, Nigeria (where some of them were re-worked). While avoiding the term "aggrey" it is clear that they considered these beads to be the genuine thing.
Along with dichroic beads in the old trading cities was a blue "corded" bead, sometimes dichroic and sometimes not, with a similar composition. They may have once been considered Aggries, too. They have also been found in Fustat (Old Cairo).
Those on the left were imported from Africa. On the strand are also dichroic blue/green beads. Are they Aggrey beads? Could be.
The anonymous Portuguese Pilot in 1640 also wrote of a false Aggrey: "some wear necklaces of glass, which are very similar [to coris ] but which will not bear the heat of fire." (Blake 1942: 153) Davison et al. found dichroic beads in newer contexts than the first group. They were heavy in lead, meaning that they would melt at a lower temperature.
These "imitation Aggrey" are well known. They are commonly called koli beads, though the Fante name for them is kor or ekeur , all three names are cognate with cori-accori-aggrey .
These beads are made by dealers in Ghana, who "cook" translucent, drawn European beads to make them opaque and change their texture (what look like "stretch marks" to some are bubbles near the surface breaking out because of the heat).
The same process is (or was) done in Nigeria to make beads called segi . It appears that pre-contact Ife, Nigeria also altered Aggrey beads, generally making them round. Aggrey beads, both altered and not, seem to have gravitated toward Ife, which accounts for so many of them coming from the Niger delta when the Europeans had to fetch them.
Left: The late Elizabeth Bruce of the famous bead dealership Teshie House in Accra, Ghana. She was 91 when I took this picture of her in 1990. She is demonstrating how she used to cook Koli beads.
Beads like the Czech ones they gave me (top right) would be packed in organic matter. A charcoal fire under the pot was lit. Elizabeth went about her work, checking on the beads and stirring them every 20 minutes or so. After about an hour, Koli beads (bottom left) would be ready to sell to their customers. From Where Beads Are Loved: Ghana, West Africa.
Koli, Cori, Kor, Segi, Accori, Ekeur, Aggrey. Lots of names to learn. Can we find genuine Aggrey beads? Possibly. One place to look might be the heirloom beads of the Asante kings. They have a blue tubular bead, but it's name doesn't sound anything like "aggrey." It is gyanie (pronounced "jenny"), after the old trading city of Jenne, no doubt. So much to learn.
References:
Blake, J. W. 1942 Europeans in West Africa 1650-1560 . London: Hakluyt Society (2 vols.) Cuyvers. Luc 1999 Into the Rising Sun: Vasco da Gama and the Search for the Sea Route to the East . New York: TV Books. Davison, Claire C., Robert D. Giauque, and J. Desmond Clark 1971 Two Chemical Groups of Dichroic Glass Beads from West Africa.

"In much the same way that the British Empire tried to take over Africa and profit from its wealth, corporations look at [teens] like this massive empire they are colonizing. And their weapons are films, music, books, CDs, internet access, clothing, amusement parks, sports teams." Robert McChesney
1. Do "cool hunters" and those who use the information they supply exploiting teens or providing desired benefits and services?
"They don't call it "human" research or "people" research, they call it "market" research."
2. Did the marketers in The Merchants of Cool get it right? Do they really know you? If MTV was really based on understanding you as a person, what would it look like?
"The MTV machine doesn't listen to the young so it can make the young happier. The MTV machine tunes in to figure out how to pitch what Viacom has to sell." Mark Crispin Miller
"Quite simply, every company with a powerful brand is attempting to develop a relationship with consumers that resonates so completely with their sense of self that they will aspire, or at least consent, to be serfs under these feudal brandlords." Naomi Klein *this quote does not appear in the video
3. Naomi Klein argues that advertising has changed from sponsoring culture to becoming culture (e.g., Sprite sponsoring a concert as opposed to Sprite becoming a component of hip hop culture). What's the difference? What happens to a culture when its purpose is sales rather than expression?
4. Distinguished scholar George Gerbner has asserted that those who control a society's stories have the power to shape the society's values. Who has control over your stories? Who do you want to have that control? Does who tells the story matter? Does MTV give an accurate voice to your stories? How does commercial control (i.e. control by those primarily interested in selling things) of media influence the kinds of stories our society hears?
5. Is the "mook" (the stereotypically crude, adolescent male) real, or just a media construction? How about the "midriff" (the girl as sex symbol)? Do you know any "mooks" or "midriffs"?
6. Do you think you or your friends are influenced by the MTV standard of "cool"? If so, how? Are there ways to be "cool" without copying media? How do the "mook" and "midriff" stereotypes relate to the corporate interests of the media outlets that perpetuate them (in other words, why these stereotypes and not some other stereotype)?
7. Many media observers have claimed that programs like Beverly Hills 90210 or Dawson's Creek are popular because they are reflective of teen life? In what ways are they reflective of how teenagers really live and in what ways are they distortions? Do these shows mirror how you live?
8. Because they do so much research, media makers think they know a lot about you. Consider whether you agree with the following assertions from TMOC. "Sex is a part of teens lives so it better be in the media too" "No teenager is going to be satisfied with a PG-13 rated horror film. They want to see blood and guts. That's what they want to do."

Marco in his Xmas hat wishes you all Season's Greeting.
10/17/05 - I have never seen a dog so interested in the making of Apple Pie. He literally stood there the entire time and watched. If Thumper came near the table where the apples were being cut he would start to do this low growl. Marco has never been motivated by food this way. He really must know how good Swedish Apple Pie is!
9/13/05 - So this is a new one for me filling out the adopted dog form I figured everyone would love to know how Marco is doing... The answer is fabulous of course. Marco has been here so long that I forget he was not really officially ours till recently. What can I say about Marco that I haven't said about him already. He is great we love him. He fits in perfectly with us. Soccer season has started and I am going to attempt to take him to a game. I fear his love of the sport may get the better of all of us though. I'll let you know how it goes. Thank you so much all of you. We are going to cherish whatever time we have with Marco.
8/24/05 - Marco's foster family has decided to adopt him and give a loving home.
8/19/05 - So now you have all heard about my babies condition the thing is no one would know that Marco had any problems unless you're told. He is a perfectly happy dog and really has no idea that these things are wrong with his body. People that see Marco on the street and in the vets office remark how cute he is and can't believe he is in rescue to begin with. We of course adore Marco and it will be a really sad time for my whole family when he leaves. We have had Marco since he was 16 weeks old and he turns one next month. Marco continues to be an outstanding dog with our Maddie who is 6 and of course the kids and any other dog he meets. He loved having the company of Newport who's energy was a great match for Marco and Marco was always surprisingly gentle with him it's like he knew he was a baby. Tonight my daughter who is 3 fed the dog's dinner and Marco was in her way and she so bluntly told him and he backed right up for her so she could get the food. When she went to dump it in his bowl she told him to sit and stay he did. Now this is a dog that is very well behaved in my book if he takes commands from a 3 year old. He never has accidents in the house and actually when I was at work today and David was sleeping he knocked down the baby gate so he could get upstairs to ask to go out. So in my book that also means he is very smart normally I don't work on days when David has gone to work the night before but I had a babysitter and it is the last week of school so I went in. Marco is good for about 4 hours then he needs to go out he sleeps in a crate all night and keeps it relatively dry. Since he has started leaking a little. This leaking started about a month ago. Marco really is a wonderful dog please don't pass him up for his problems. He really doesn't know he has them and he is a great companion.
8/18/05 - Marco has been leaking urine and was brought into the vet. A urinalysis was done and no infection was noted. However, the vet realized that the urine was not concentrated enough and did a renal ultrasound to rule out any kidney problems. Unfortunately tests results showed that Marco's kidneys were not of normal shape or size. He was diagnosed with congenital renal dysphasia. There is no cure for this disease. Medication can control any leaking, but if Marco is let out often, he does not leak. His kidney function at this time is normal, as blood work was done to confirm that. Eventually his kidneys will not function. The vet said it was hard to say when it will happen, but guessed that is could happen within months or years. This poor little guy has no idea that he has this. He plays non-stop and is full of energy. A special person is needed for our Marco. Ideally someone that is home so Marco could be let out frequently and who has lots of love to give. If any Angel is out there who is interested, please fill out an application
8/7/05 - So I know everyone is wondering why Marco is back up on the available dogs list :( Sadly we are finding ourselves in a position that we are not able to adopt Marco. This truly breaks my heart as I feel as if he is our dog. He has been with us for nearly 8 months and surprisingly turns a year old next month. Sept 29th to be exact. My husband is due to go away for the next year leaving me home with our kids, running the house and unable to work, seeing him only on weekends as long as the weather permits. Please don't think this is any reflection on Marco we couldn't ask for a better dog and neither could you. He is extremely well behaved. For example yesterday the gate to the back yard was open and Marco didn't even think about leaving he stood in the back yard till we came back and closed the gate. He knows all of his basic commands and is so gentle. He gets very excited when company comes but rarely jumps and is easily corrected. He turns his boxer butt inside out at the sight of company. He continues to sleep in the crate at night but has free run of the house during the day with Maddie. Marco would love a companion someone who can keep up with him or an older dog who is young at heart and willing to play. Marco gets along with everyone. I am sure he would love some human kids to play with as well as he is marvelous with our kids ages 7 and 3. He is super gentle and careful not to knock anyone over not to say that doesn't happen if he is running full speed for something. Of course his favorite past time is still playing ball. Honest and truly I can't even believe that Marco has to move on but we foster for a reason. He is part of our family and whomever is lucky enough to adopt him wont regret it. His colitis is under control at the moment and the vet says it looks really good. There is a chance that he will have flare ups but they can be treated. Please consider adopting my sweet baby boy.
7/2/05 - Wow sure time flies when your having fun. Marco is doing really well on his meds after being diagnosed with colitis. He continues to be an amazing dog and faithful companion. He was very good after my jaw surgery he really knew I was down and out and he was very caring and also took care to leave me alone and not ask me to play. The other day I said ouch about something and he came up and started licking me as if to say "hey it's OK" He also does this cute little thing as a sign of affection were he nibbles on you. He not only does this to us but to Maddie as well. He is so stinkin cute. I cant believe how big he is. Marco has gained 10 pounds since his last vet visit which was June 1st. So ten pounds in a month not to shabby. He is still only eating twice a day but I think he must be digesting better and keeping more nutrients in his body. With this update I am sending new pictures so everyone can see how big he is getting.
6/14/05 - Thought I'd send in an update to let you all know that Marco is doing well. He is being treated with an antibiotic for his colitis and seems to be doing really well. We should really start seeing a difference by the end of the week or the beginning of next week according to his Dr's. Marco continues to be an excellent family dog. He loves the kids and our queen Maddie. Of course his favorite thing to do to her still is nibble her neck. Marco still loves to play ball by far his favorite game. He has taken to chewing sticks and always manages to sneak one in to chew on. I am assuming he does this when the kids let him in. Marco loves other animals and his mission in life is to play with every single one. This has started walking a little more difficult as he cannot just pass by he would like to stop and say hi. A quick reminder to keep walking and a tug on the leash and he comes. I truly am glad I have watched him grow and he is going to be with us for awhile longer till he finishes his antibiotics.
6/3/05 - I wanted to let everyone know Marco is doing well. On Wednesday he went in for his nueter as well as his colonoscopy. If you ask him though it was like he was never there. He came out of the vets office like who's here to play! We will know his results next week and I am very excited to find out what is going on with our little man who is not so little anymore. I was on the phone with a moving company and they heard Marco barking and they stopped the conversation to say wow that sounds like a big dog you have there. If only they could see this goofy boy on the other end. He is also so smart and well behaved. He follows us outside in the am when we walk to school and stands at the gate. I tell him to go wait in the corner because you can see us walking home from that part of the yard and he automatically goes to the corner to wait. As I cross the street I say there's my baby and he heads over to meet at the gate. Another great thing about Marco is he wont leave out the front door unless he has permission. He waits till you tell him its OK. It's going to be a very sad day for us when Marco leaves. We have had him for such a long time and he was only a baby when he came now he is almost as tall as Maddie.
4/21/05 - Hey Everybody just wanted to give a little update on Marco since he is still here with us. We are still trying to figure out what is going on with his bowels it's been a long process but sometimes these things are. I really want to talk about how great Marco is. I know he has a family patiently waiting for him but I figured other people might want to read about our little man. First of all Spring has really Sprung here in upstate NY the weather has been great and Marco loves to be outside. He doesn't even care if he is back there by himself he will stand at the fence and just watch everything that is going on. We can leave our back door open and Marco will push the porch door open to let himself out. Marco is also very good about not taking off when the kids open the front door. He does not try to escape. Marco went to the vet yesterday and met a lot of dogs big and small. The first dog he met was on the dog walk he was a young but big male black lab. The second dog he met was the labs sister who was a Great Prynese. Marco stood perfectly still and let this giant dog sniff him all over she even lifted him up when she sniffed under his belly. When all was said and down and she was done sniffing Marco proceeded to jump onto her back. I really think he thought it was play time because he was a good boy and let the dog do what she wanted. Marco has no dog aggression at all he really just wants to play play play. He doesn't seem to realize that everyone he meets doesn't want to play with him. He was also perfect for the tech who drew his blood. David (my husband and Marco's foster Dad) was holding him on the table and the tech was standing in front of Marco drawing his blood from his front leg and he was licking her face. She was so impressed at how good he was she went to find him some special treats. Marco is a great dog and I just wanted to let everyone know how lucky we feel to have him despite the potty training which is just something we haven't done in a long time. Marco is a perfect companion for grownups and kids. When things get figured out and it is time for him to go he will truly be missed. Marco has been with us for nearly 4 months and we have watched him grow and continue to blossom. In my opinion Marco is an outstanding representation of the Boxer Breed.
3/31/05 - We just wanted to let everyone know that Marco is doing great. When he first came I wasn't sure I was going to get through the whole potty training thing as it has been a long time since we trained a dog. For the life of me I couldn't figure out why he would go outside and then 10 minutes later go in the house. Thankfully those days are over. I can officially say Marco is 100% house trained. However he did go pee when I closed the dogs off in the front part of the house so a man who was working on our house could come in to go the bathroom. I wasn't worried about Marco but Maddie does not like strange people in the house so I thought it would be easier to just close the door. I guess Marco didn't like that. He also hates the rain and getting him to go out was a project and he did have an accident that day as well. Let me tell you though he is one super little guy. He is so well behaved. So much so that we stopped crating him when we go out. He has been left for 4 hours max with free run of the house(except the upstairs). He has been an absolute gentlemen not getting into a thing and not going potty in the house. Personally I think he and Maddie just veg out on the couch. Icant say for sure I would trust him if he didn't have a companion to hang out with but he has been great here. Marco loves those big playground balls and they keep him entertained for long periods of time. He pushes them around the yard with his head and rolls over top of them. He even got Maddie to join in the action yesterday and she is not really a ball kind of gal. She much prefers a good squeaky toy. As for squeaky toys the dont stand a chance with Marco he rips them apart till he gets the squeaker out. We love Marco he is an excellent dog. My daughter adores him and wants him to do everything with her. When it is time to do something like get dressed or do her hair she always wants Marco to sit with her. If he happens to sit where she isn't she gets right up and goes to where he is. It is going to be sad for all of us when Marco leaves but I know some family is going to enjoy him as much as we do. And hopefully he will bring as many smiles to another littles face as he does to my daughters. Unfortunately Marco is still not ready to go to his forever home as his poop issues aren't cleared up yet. Believe me when I say he is worth the wait :)
3/11/05 - Hi everyone I know that I don't technically have to update Marco since he is pending adoption and all :) but we wanted to let everyone know how fabulous he is doing with his potty training. We actually went a few days with only one accident in the house the other days we had none. I decided to take the big plunge today and let Marco stay out of the crate. I knew I wasn't going to be gone long. I also thought to myself what is the worst that is going to happen that he pees somewhere. It's not like it would be something new or unexpected. I was gone 45 minutes and to my pleasure Marco was a perfect little gentleman when I was gone. I was extremely proud of my little baby. Just wanted to share our happy news.
3/3/05 - Marco is pending adoption
2/27/05 - Marco continues to do very well here with us. We are very impressed at how smart he is (would we expect anything less of a boxer) and how quickly he is learning. The accidents in the house are far and few between on average he has about 2 a day but now they come in streams as he is running for the back door. As soon as we open the back door he runs to the porch door and pushes it open. He continues to do well with the resident animals and has also played with my cousins dog who lives across the street. My cousins dog is a very strong dominant dog with a lot of herding instincts due to his breed type but they played really well together which I have to say surprised me not because of Marco even though his puppy antics are not for every dog. Marco is an absolute joy to have around. I really found out this week after only being gone for 2 days how much I really love this little guy and how I truly missed him and couldn't wait to see he and Maddie both. My Mom came to stay with the queen and the little prince so my family and I could take a mini vacation. My Mom just adores Marco and since she lives right down the street she sees him just about every day. Marco continues to bark when people come to the door and at 18 weeks old it is a very menacing bark. I wonder what it will be like when he gets older as it sounds so grown up now. He is also getting more vocal in his play which we are trying to curb. He still adores my little girl and I could send you a thousand pictures of the 2 of them together. The only time he gets slightly annoyed with her is if she is squeezing him a little to much for his liking. We speak to them both firmly after that him for getting snappy even though it is just his way of telling her to stop and to her about how she is making Marco feel when she does that. However you can always find the 2 of them together. Maddie is also starting to instigate a lot more play sessions which I find amusing. She seems to do this with our younger fosters when they have been he awhile. Marco will be trying to relax and Maddie will come over and punch him usually in the head and then she will start whining at him to get up and play. Of course he is more then willing to oblige. Marco is a beautiful dog I think he is going to be rather large when he is full grown. We love our baby Marco Polo but just like all the other dogs he deserves a home to call his own. Marco is waiting :)
2/22/05 - What a joy having a puppy brings into the house! Despite having to clean up after him here and there Marco is such a good baby. He is very gentle and is learning all his good puppy manners. Yesterday we went for a ride in the car which he loves and he stayed in the back with Maddie. He was so funny chasing the windshield wiper on the back window. Marco plays very well with Maddie and she truly is loving and accepting of him as well. He doesn't bother with the cat. He goes into his crate with no problems and sleeps perfectly through the night. Marco thoroughly enjoys playing ball sometimes he is so funny because his and eye coordination isn't the greatest and he looses the ball so then we send one of the kids to retrieve it. Speaking of kids I know I have mentioned it before but Marco adores my little girl who will be 3 in April. The 2 of them are like peas in pod and always together. This makes my son a little jealous because he tries so hard to get Marco to sit with him. I envision a home for Marco one much like my own. With adult humans who are home more often then not and kids and another dog to play with. He would thrive in a home where he got lots of attention and a family who will continue working on all his good puppy skills. We are giving Marco another round of antibiotics so say a prayer for us that this round finally clears things up for him. How can you resist this little guy. He is a beautiful boy who is going to be even more beautiful as he grows. I will love to follow his updates and see his pictures as he grows. Having Marco here is like having another baby to love and nurture and help grow into a perfect companion.
2/17/05 - We have had Marco for some time now with secret hopes of being able to adopt him ourselves (who can resist a puppy) after lengthy discussions we decided to let someone else have the pleasure. This is in no part to do with Marco as a dog because he is great. He is working really hard on his house training and we have discovered if we only give him water with his meals and of course after a serious playtime he does much better. He only had one accident in the house yesterday and has not had any yet today. Marco continues to get along great with Maddie and he leaves the cat alone. He seems to be interested but rather not test those waters and if he starts to run after her to follow where she is going we just tell him "no Kitty" and he stops. Marco is great with the kids ages 6 and almost 3. He loves my youngest and follows her everywhere. By far his favorite thing to do is play ball he will play forever if you will. He is also very calm for a puppy a lot of times he is perfectly content to find a sunny spot on the rug and sun himself. He keeps his crate dry at night and doesn't mind going in there. The other day when we went out though I put him in his crate and he peed and mind you this was after he just went out. He went out went to the bathroom came in got a treat went in the crate and got another treat ate it then peed. He does bark when someone comes to the door a behavior I am sure he learned from our Maddie. He does not dig and really hasn't destroyed anything either. He is very easily redirected when he does take something that doesn't belong to him. Another one of his favorite things to do is dump out the toy buckets. I am forever picking those things up. Marco walks great on the leash and stays in the yard when told. Marco cam into rescue being treated for packed anal glands and bloody stool. He has seen the vet here and has had 2 more rounds of antibiotics for what they believed to be bacterial colitis however the antibiotics did not clear up this problem. Marco will require further testing to see what is going on there. Unfortunately we see stuff like this all to often when puppies are purchased from pet stores or backyard breeders. People who are out for money and not for the health and temperament of the dog. Marco is a sweet dog and we all love him dearly he is a very handsome little guy. I have taken tons and tons of pictures of him. We really love having him her and will continue to feel blessed by his presence for as long as he is here. Please consider Marco as your forever boxer baby.
2/11/05 - I am going a little crazy with Marco and the potty training. We let him out constantly as we are both home more often then not. He goes when let out and we praise him and even give him treats. He can go for a long time without going to the bathroom as he can hold it all night and does because his crate his always dry. He drinks a lot and it is like it goes right through him you can hear him drinking in the kitchen and then you hear him peeing. Literally as soon as he steps away from the water bowl he pees even if he was just outside. I am really happy he is not going on my carpets anymore but we have been going through paper towels like mad. He looks at you like he knows he is wrong and we just show him the pee and tell him no outside and then send him out and as soon as he comes back in he sits for a treat and I tell him no because he went pee in the house. He is still a baby only 16 weeks old and I did realize I would be doing a lot of cleaning and I have no problem with that I guess I just don't understand why he can hold it all night and not during the day. Not that I am expecting him to hold it all day but as I said before he can go out and do his business and 20 minutes later he is peeing in the house. But we absolutely adore Marco and he is very smart. He is doing extremely well honestly. He loves to play ball and he and Maddie are great friends. He even started sleeping on her couch with her. When I walk him to school people are surprised how he has grown in the 2 weeks he has been here. It has been a long time since we had a puppy as Maddie will be 6 in April. He is just very inconsistent with his potty habits. It seems like he will go all day without going in the house and then by evening he is going all the time. I wonder if it is because our house gets busier once the school day is over. We will just keep working on it and pray this last round of antibiotics clears up any other issues.
2/4/05 - Hi everyone just wanted to let you all know Marco is doing well and is continuing to get better with his antibiotics. He is a cool little dog and so smart. He is also very well behaved. Last night after Marco at his dinner he went right into the other room to lay down without even being told. He didn't want to go in the crate like the night before I think. He is super in the crate and keeps it dry all night and also if we have to go out. We are still working on the house breaking sometimes he is really good and other times not. It is like potty training which we are going through with our daughter. All in all Marco is a great dog he has the cutest little under bite and you can see all his perfect puppy teeth straight as can be. We will continue with the antibiotics for a while longer so hopefully he will be 100%. Till next time.
2/1/05 - Just wanted to do a little write up about Marco. Marco or "baby Marco Polo" as we call him is doing great. He has been here a week and is learning so much for instance it makes Mommy less upset if I pee in the kitchen instead of on her carpet. He is also starting to scratch at the back door when he wants out or in. My sister thinks I should teach him how to ring a bell that hangs from the door knob. He walks great on a leash for such a young pup and since he is so young and we live on a quite dead end street we are teaching him how to stay in the yard. He is a master at sit and he sits for everything. He sits for his treats, he sits and waits for you to put down his food and water. Marco is also very good about going in the crate. Although he probably rather not I really don't want him peeing on my new carpets upstairs of course I don't expect such a young guy to be potty trained and he gets a stern no and then told to go outside but if using the crate means saving my carpets from pee. Then the crate I will use. He is very good about it though I tell him to crate up and he goes in. He whines for maybe 5 seconds but I just say shhhhh it's time for night night and he stops. He gets along great with Maddie and plays with her a lot differently then he did with Sabrina he is a lot more gentle. He has realized the cat is a force to be reckoned with and even though he is interested he usually doesn't go near her. Tasha however will come to him when he is calm she doesn't view him as a threat. He absolutely adores my little girl and follows her everywhere. She calls him baby. He is a great little guy and so handsome. I love having him here and I would love to keep him. We were going to wait till Maddie got a little older but have started thinking about it more seriously since someone is home all the time and in a couple of years I will go back to work full time. Also for a puppy he is not destructive I don't know if he hasn't reached that stage yet but even when he picks up one of the kids toys he is easily redirected. Of course right now he is sliding one of my daughters plastic plates around the house. He is a super little guy.
1/24/05 - The Mayor's Alliance van dropped off 3 Boxers here to me for a brief visit. Good as gold all three. China is a girl who likes to give me a hug. My son and his friend named the big boy with the Spike (later renamed Jack by his foster home, and of course the baby Marco who were are going to foster.

As China continues to invest millions of dollars in Africa, and Beijing boosts its aid package to the continent, hundreds of Chinese-owned businesses have established themselves throughout Africa. But there’ve been widespread allegations that the Chinese treat their workers very badly, paying them poorly and forcing them to work long hours in sometimes dangerous conditions. Many Africans have also lost their jobs in textile factories, as a result of cheap Chinese imports and labor. This has resulted in violent anti-Chinese protests in Zambia, for example. China has also been criticized for selling weapons to administrations accused of human rights violations in Africa. But there’s also some optimism surrounding China’s role on the continent. In the final part of a series focusing on China in Africa, VOA’s Darren Taylor reports on growing admiration amongst Africans for Chinese people making their living on the continent.
In many parts of Africa, the Chinese are admired for their resilience and hard work, says Prof. Deborah Brautigam of American University’s School of International Service, who has traveled to China and throughout Africa since the 1980’s to study Beijing’s changing relationship with the continent.
Despite the negative aspects of China’s increasing involvement in Africa - such as allegations that it’s out to plunder the continent of its natural resources, ignores human rights concerns and “dumps” its cheap products on Africa - the country’s nationals who’ve established themselves on the continent have also garnered great respect.
The Chinese are particularly admired, says Brautigam, for their frugal living habits.
In recent years, China has exported a variety of experts to support Africa’s development, and Chinese contractors have built infrastructure all over the continent. But, in contrast with Western expatriates, says Brautigam, the Chinese always live at or below “local standards” - even when it’s quite within their means to live lavishly.
“Some of these (Chinese) experts living at local standards have led local people to think that they must be convicts, because who else would live like that? They (usually) live way below local standards, let’s put it that way,” Brautigam says.
She adds that while the focus of China’s increased involvement in Africa has been on the entrance of large Chinese businesses into the continent, an unreported aspect of the relationship is the “way in which Chinese small-scale traders going into Africa impacts on perceptions of China in Africa.”
According to Brautigam, a large part of why China’s appeal has grown throughout Africa “has to do just with the on-the-ground comparison between Chinese businesspeople, and Western expatriates.”
Joshua Kurlantzick, a fellow at the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace and expert on Chinese economic policy, agrees.
“I can’t count the number of times I spoke with people in South Africa or Kenya or Mozambique, who explicitly made the comparison between Chinese traders – some of whom had actually been sleeping in the market – and Western expatriates…who are viewed very differently than small-scale Chinese traders.”
Many Western expatriates in Africa live in relative luxury, and are resented by some Africans for their apparent efforts to isolate themselves from the communities amongst which they work.
The Chinese also don’t interact very much with local Africans, Brautigam acknowledges.
“One of the problems I think with the way that the Chinese invest and do their aid in Africa is that it’s very much in an enclave. The Chinese live together, they construct their own compounds…These are enclaves where a lot of Chinese businesses set up together and they’re much more comfortable like that and I think this is something that the Chinese need to think of breaking out of.”
But according to Brautigam, China’s nationals working in Africa have nevertheless gained more respect on the continent because of their simple way of living. She uses a practical example she witnessed in the recent past to give credence to her point of view.
In Sierra Leone, explains Brautigam, the Chinese established a hydropower project, as did the Italians, and both projects were staffed by expatriates.
“The Chinese lived in these very simple, zinc-like township houses - very simple temporary structures, like dormitories. They all lived there. They worked all day long, and at the end of the day, they had to come back and they had to go to their vegetable farm. They all had to supply a certain amount of vegetables for their communal meals in the evening…Each one had to harvest the vegetables, bring them to the cook and the cook would make the meals.”
But Brautigam says the experts at the Italian hydropower project lived very differently.
“The Italian project had a container of food brought in from Italy every two weeks! And the (Italian) experts were living in very comfortable houses that were built (specially) for the project.”
At another agricultural research scheme in Sierra Leone, says Brautigam, 12 Chinese experts lived “for years” in a simple building that they eventually vacated for their hosts to use as a storage facility for fresh produce.
She contrasts the behavior of the Chinese agricultural experts with that of a group of American advisors, who’d established a similar project in West Africa.
“The United States…had eight experts and they had built ranch-style houses in a little subdivision, with street lights and sidewalks, and everything the way it would be in Texas or someplace like that!” Brautigam laughs.
While Western donors tend to spend a lot of their aid funds on luxurious housing and high salaries for their experts, and anything that’ll provide comfort for their expatriate staff in the host country, the Chinese prefer to spend the money on the actual aid projects, she says.
“These (types of behavior) have implications in terms of the bang for the buck. If you’re building all those ranch-style houses, and flying in containers of food, your aid money doesn’t go as far,” Brautigam quips.
Ndubisi Obiorah, the Executive Director of Nigeria’s Centre for Law and Social Action, says one of the major reasons for China’s popularity amongst African political and business leaders is the country’s provision of expatriates who provide cheaper expertise to the continent.
In Nigeria, he says, the Chinese are perceived as being “better able to transfer technology to Nigerian employees than Western expatriates.”
In Obiorah’s experience, China is popular amongst businesspeople in Africa because of a simple reason: Money.
“Africans associate the Chinese with profits,” says Obiorah.
Kurlantzick says his research, which has focused upon perceptions of China in Africa, shows that the Chinese remain essentially popular on the continent, despite negative publicity such as the flood of cheap Chinese labor and goods into Africa. He says Africans have a “very favorable impression” of China largely because of the country’s eagerness to build essential infrastructure, such as roads, across the continent, which they consider essential to their attempts to escape from poverty.

Besides fighting, drinking and plundering, pirates are known for making music!!!! Every pirate loves to sing (though, you may not love to hear him!) But there are some musical pirates that are a delight to listen to. The Corsairs gave us many hours of entertainment at the Pirates in Paradise Festival last year, and we were glad to see they made it again this year. Besides the Corsairs, we were thrilled the Pyrates Royale made it this time, as well as the lads & lasses from the NY Ren Faire, who call themselves the Crimson Pirates. Other groups sang as well, including members of On the Lamb and the Pyrates of Tortuga. But a pirate doesn't have to belong to an official musical troupe in order to entertain himself and others with song. In fact ... just try and stop him (or her).
Pirates will break into song, anywhere, anytime, at the drop of a doubloon (Red Dog, Winchester Crumbee and Bartholomew Roberts)
(Bartholomew Roberts, Mark Miller, Bawdy Be and ?)
Many musicians take a break here on the docks. (One of the Corsettes, two of the Corsairs with another Corsette behind them, Mark Miller with concertina, Michael Noles with bodhran, and Winchester Crumbee)
Musicians and their instruments Left: One of the Crimson Pirates waits on the dock with her guitar case Right: A local, non-pirate brings his own drum and just joins in.
